1: ἐμαστίγωσεν -- the verb is the standard term for Roman flagellatio, flogging with a leather scourge weighted with lead or bone. This was distinct from the Jewish synagogue punishment of thirty-nine lashes. Roman flogging was not regulated by Jewish law and could be lethal. The Evangelist records it in a single verb -- the severity is implied, not narrated.
2: ἐκ ἀκανθῶν στέφανον -- a crown of thorns (akanthon stephanon). The word stephanon is the victor's or ruler's wreath, not the diadem of royalty. The soldiers use the symbol of honor to enact mockery; the Evangelist lets the irony speak without comment. The purple (porfyroûn) robe signifies royalty and maps onto the imperial purple -- soldiers likely commandeered a legionary cloak.
3: ἐρράπιζον -- the imperfect tense marks repeated action: they kept striking. The mocking address "Hail, King of the Jews" (Chairé, ho Basileùs tōn Ioudaíōn) directly parodies the imperial salute Ave Caesar. The irony the Evangelist sustains throughout the trial scenes is intensified here: the soldiers performing mockery are, unknowingly, enacting a throne-room investiture.
4: οὐδεμίαν αἰτίαν εὑρίσκω -- "I find no charge" (aitían). This is Pilate's third formal declaration of innocence (cf. John 18:38; John 19:6). The triple-repeated acquittal by Rome's representative is structurally significant: it functions as a legal refrain. The Evangelist uses the verb heuriskō (find by investigation) rather than a simple negative assertion -- Pilate has looked and concluded.
5: Ἰδοὺ ὁ ἄνθρωπος -- "Behold the man!" This famous pronouncement (Ecce homo) is unique to John. The phrase is ambiguous: Pilate intends it as an appeal to pity, but the Evangelist's presentation of Jesus as the Logos-made-flesh gives ho anthrōpos its fullest weight -- this is the human one, in whom God is disclosed. The same Idou construction echoes the Baptist's "Behold, the Lamb of God" (John 1:36).
6: Σταύρωσον σταύρωσον -- the double imperative without connective (asyndeton) conveys urgency and crowd pressure. Pilate's response -- "take him yourselves and crucify him" -- is a taunt: Roman law gave Jews no authority to execute. The formal capitulation does not come until John 19:16. The repetition of ouk heuriskō (I find no charge) is the Evangelist's fourth record of this verdict within two chapters.
7: υἱὸν θεοῦ -- "Son of God" -- the charge now shifts from political to blasphemy. The definite article is absent (huion theou, not ton huion tou theou), though in context the anarthrous construction does not necessarily denote a lesser claim. The Jewish leaders cite their law -- katà tòn nómon hēmōn -- invoking Leviticus 24:16 (blasphemy deserves death). This is the charge that produces Pilate's fear in John 19:8.
8: μᾶλλον ἐφοβήθη -- "was more afraid" -- the comparative mâllon signals that Pilate was already afraid (cf. the repeated attempts to release Jesus), and the charge of divine sonship deepens it. The Evangelist does not explain Pilate's fear; its cause is left open. Whether superstitious dread of a divine figure, political caution, or something else, the narrative withholds psychological closure.
9: πόθεν εἶ σύ -- "Where are you from?" The question is one the Evangelist has already answered for the reader (John 1:1-14; John 8:23) but Pilate cannot know. Jesus's silence -- apokrisin ouk edōken, gave no answer -- echoes the Servant of Isaiah 53:7 ("he did not open his mouth"). The refusal to answer Pilate stands in contrast to Jesus's extensive dialogue with him in John 18:33-38.
10: ἐξουσίαν ἔχω -- Pilate twice claims exousían (authority, delegated right) -- to release and to crucify. The word recurs in John 19:11 where Jesus subordinates it. Exousía is not raw capacity but official standing; Pilate's error is treating it as autonomous rather than derived.
11: ἄνωθεν -- "from above" -- the same word used in 3:3 and John 3:31. Pilate's authority is delegated from God (anōthen), not self-derived. Jesus's statement does not acquit Pilate; it calibrates guilt. The one who delivered Jesus to Pilate (ho paradous) bears the greater sin -- the reference is ambiguous (Caiaphas? Judas? the chief priests as a group?) and the Evangelist likely intends the ambiguity to be felt.
12: φίλος τοῦ Καίσαρος -- "friend of Caesar" (Kaisaros philos) was a formal designation of loyalty and favor, not merely a colloquial term. Losing this status had real political and economic consequences. The accusation -- that releasing Jesus makes Pilate disloyal to the emperor -- converts a theological dispute into a political threat. The shift in strategy (from religious charge to treason) is noted by the Evangelist without comment.
13: Γαββαθά -- Gabbathâ, the Aramaic name for the stone pavement (Lithostroton). John includes dual designations (Greek and Aramaic) as he does for Golgotha and other place names, consistent with the multilingual context of Jerusalem. The bēma (judgment seat) was the official platform for pronouncing sentences; Pilate sitting on it makes the trial formal and the verdict legally binding.
14: ὥρα ἦν ὡς ἕκτη -- "about the sixth hour" -- approximately noon. This creates a tension with Mark 15:25, which places the crucifixion at the third hour. Various harmonization attempts exist (different reckoning systems; "about" absorbing the gap). The Evangelist may also have a theological interest: noon is when the Passover lambs began to be slaughtered in the temple. The resonance with 1:29 and 19:36 is available to the reader.
15: Οὐκ ἔχομεν βασιλέα εἰ μὴ Καίσαρα -- "We have no king but Caesar." The Evangelist presents this as the climactic self-declaration of the chief priests. In Jewish theological terms, YHWH alone is Israel's king (1 Samuel 8:7; Isaiah 26:13). The priests' answer formally repudiates that kingship. The statement is theologically catastrophic in a way that the narrative renders plainly; no commentary is added by the Evangelist -- the irony is structural.
16: παρέδωκεν αὐτὸν -- "handed him over" -- the verb paradidōmi has appeared throughout the passion narrative for Judas's betrayal (John 6:71; John 13:2) and now for Pilate's capitulation. The repeated handover language signals a chain of delegation: Judas to the priests, priests to Pilate, Pilate to the soldiers. Against the backdrop of Jesus's own words (John 10:18: "I lay down my life of myself") the Evangelist sustains the paradox -- the one delivered controls the delivery.
17: βαστάζων ἑαυτῷ τὸν σταυρόν -- "bearing his cross himself" -- the Evangelist omits Simon of Cyrene (present in all three Synoptics). The deliberate use of heautō (for himself) foregrounds Jesus's agency. In context of John 12:24-26 (where taking up the cross is discipleship language) the Evangelist presents the scene as an enacted parable of what Jesus has been teaching.
18: ἐσταύρωσαν -- the verb is aorist; the act is stated without elaboration. The Evangelist does not describe the physical procedure of crucifixion. What he notes is position: Jesus in the middle (meson), the two others on either side. The placement echoes no single text but the repeated centrality of Jesus in the passion narrative frames the detail theologically.
19: τίτλον -- the Latin loanword titlon (titulus) was the wooden board listing the criminal's name and offense, carried before the prisoner or affixed to the cross. Its content -- JESUS OF NAZARETH, THE KING OF THE JEWS -- is identical across all four Gospels with minor variation. The Evangelist names Pilate as the author (egrapse, wrote), placing the ironic proclamation as Pilate's formal act.
20: Ἑβραϊστί, Ῥωμαϊστί, Ἑλληνιστί -- Aramaic, Latin, and Greek. The three languages represent the entire cultural world of the province: Jewish religion, Roman law, Greek culture. The trilingual proclamation of Jesus as king over all three spheres is noted only in John. The location's proximity to the city meant the inscription was read by many -- it was, in effect, a public announcement.
21: μὴ γράφε -- "do not write" -- present imperative with mē stops an ongoing action: the inscription already stands. The chief priests want it revised to a reported claim, not a declaration. Pilate's refusal signals -- whatever his intent -- the Evangelist's conviction that what is written stands.
22: Ὃ γέγραφα γέγραφα -- "What I have written, I have written." The repeated perfect tense (gegráphe -- "it stands written") conveys finality: a past act whose result endures. The Greek perfect is the Evangelist's tense for permanent, irreversible action (cf. John 19:28; John 20:23). The phrase has become proverbial for irrevocability; the Evangelist gives Pilate the last word because it is, in theological terms, the correct answer.
23: ἄραφος -- "without seam, woven from the top throughout" -- the seamless garment. The detail is unique to John. Josephus describes the high-priestly robe as similarly seamless (Ant. 3.161); the Evangelist's audience would register the resonance without needing it spelled out. The soldiers' refusal to divide it -- mē schisōmen, let us not tear -- fulfills Psalms 22:18, cited in John 19:24.
24: ἵνα ἡ γραφὴ πληρωθῇ -- fulfillment formula. The citation is Psalms 22:18 (LXX John 21:19): "they divided my garments among them and cast lots for my clothing." The Evangelist uses the fulfillment formula more densely in the passion than anywhere else in the Gospel. The two-part action -- dividing the garments and casting lots -- corresponds to the two clauses of the psalm, which is Hebrew synonymous parallelism. The soldiers enact both clauses literally.
25: εἱστήκεισαν δὲ παρὰ τῷ σταυρῷ -- "standing by the cross" -- the pluperfect heistēkeisan indicates sustained presence; they had been standing there and continued to stand. Four women are named, though the relationship of "his mother's sister" to "Mary the wife of Clopas" is disputed (two women or four?). The contrast with the male disciples who fled (John 18:8) is implied but not stated.
26: Γύναι, ἴδε ὁ υἱός σου -- "Woman, behold your son." The address Gynai (Woman) is the same form used at Cana (John 2:4) and marks a significant moment in both cases. It is not a diminishment in Greek; it is a formal address of a social equal. The Evangelist notes the presence of the Beloved Disciple here for the transfer of filial responsibility -- a legal and social act carried out from the cross.
27: εἰς τὰ ἴδια -- "into his own home" (or "his own things/household"). The phrase is identical to John 1:11 -- "his own people did not receive him" -- a deliberate echo: the Beloved Disciple receives what the nation did not. The taking in of Jesus's mother is both a practical provision and a theological statement about the community formed by the cross.
28: τετέλεσται -- "it is finished/accomplished" -- here the Evangelist uses a subordinated clause: "knowing that all things were now finished" (ēdē panta tetelestai). The verb teleioō (John 16:23; John 17:4) has been used for completion of the Father's work; now teleō (G5055) marks the discharge of an obligation. Διψῶ -- "I thirst" -- stated so that scripture might be fulfilled; identified with Psalms 22:15 and Psalms 69:21 (vinegar given to the thirsty).
29: ὑσσώπῳ -- hyssop (hyssōpō). The detail is significant: in Exodus 12:22 hyssop is the instrument for applying the Passover blood to the doorposts. John's passion narrative has been structured around Passover throughout; the hyssop at the cross draws the line back to the Exodus deliverance. Mark and Matthew specify a reed (kalamos); the Evangelist's choice of hyssop is almost certainly deliberate.
30:
Τετέλεσται -- "It is finished." The verb teleō (G5055) means to complete, discharge, or conclude -- particularly the discharge of a debt or obligation. The word was used in commercial and legal contexts for a payment fully made. The Evangelist's single Greek word is the theological apex of the passion narrative: everything the Father commissioned (John 4:34; John 17:4) is now brought to its end.

παρέδωκεν τὸ πνεῦμα -- "handed over the spirit." The verb paradidōmi is the same chain-of-handover word used for Judas, for Pilate, and now here. The expression for dying in Greek idiom is typically ekpneō (breathe out) or aphiēmi; paradidōmi implies a deliberate act of transmission. Whether the Evangelist intends a reference to the Spirit given to the disciples (cf. John 20:22) is debated, but the choice of verb is not accidental.
31:
παρασκευή -- "preparation" (day before the Sabbath). The Evangelist's chronology places the death on Preparation Day, making it Passover Eve (14 Nisan) -- distinct from the Synoptics where the Last Supper appears to be the Passover meal. For the Evangelist, Jesus dies as the lambs are being prepared, which coheres with 1:29 and the structuring of the passion around the Passover lamb.

μεγάλη ἡ ἡμέρα -- "great day" -- this Sabbath coincided with Passover, making it doubly significant. The request to break legs (kataxōsin) and remove bodies is motivated by Deuteronomy 21:22-23 (a hanged man's body must not remain overnight).
32: Transitional narrative. The soldiers break the legs of the two crucified with Jesus. The Evangelist notes this to establish the contrast with what they find when they come to Jesus in John 19:33.
33: ἤδη αὐτὸν τεθνηκότα -- "already dead" -- the perfect participle tethnēkota marks the completed state of death. The Evangelist underlines that Jesus was verifiably dead before the spear thrust; there is no ambiguity. The soldiers (experienced executioners) confirm the fact by not breaking his legs.
34: αἷμα καὶ ὕδωρ -- "blood and water." The Evangelist records both fluid components in a specific order. Various medical explanations have been proposed (pericardial effusion; post-mortem separation of blood). But the Evangelist frames this as theologically significant through the eyewitness note in John 19:35. The sacramental reading (Eucharist and baptism; cf. 1 John 5:6-8) is present in early Christian interpretation. The order -- blood first, then water -- is the same as 1 John 5:6: "not by water only but by water and blood."
35: ὁ ἑωρακὼς μεμαρτύρηκεν -- "the one who has seen has testified." Both verbs are perfect tense: the seeing is a completed act whose result stands; the testimony is ongoing. The Evangelist speaks of himself in the third person -- consistent with his practice throughout (John 13:23; John 19:26; John 21:7; John 21:20). The emphatic truthfulness claim (alēthinē estin hē martyria autou) has the character of a legal deposition, inserted precisely at the theologically loaded detail of blood and water.
36: ὀστοῦν οὐ συντριβήσεται αὐτοῦ -- "a bone of him shall not be broken." The citation is from Exodus 12:46 (and Numbers 9:12), the Passover lamb regulation. This is the only place in the NT where the Passover lamb connection is made explicit by citation rather than by typological implication. The fulfillment language (hina plērōthēi) frames it as deliberate divine design.
37: ὄψονται εἰς ὃν ἐξεκέντησαν -- "they shall look on him whom they pierced." The citation is from Zechariah 12:10. The Evangelist follows a reading closer to the MT than the LXX (which has "they shall mourn for him"). The verb ekkenteín (to pierce through) is the same word used for the spear thrust in John 19:34 -- the translator's word choice made the connection between Zechariah and the passion scene verbally precise.
38: κεκρυμμένος δὲ διὰ τὸν φόβον τῶν Ἰουδαίων -- "hidden because of the fear of the Jewish leaders." Joseph of Arimathea is introduced as a disciple (mathētēs) but a secret one. The Evangelist uses the same language as John 12:42 (rulers who believed but did not confess "because of the Pharisees"). His public act of requesting the body amounts to the confession that his secrecy had previously prevented. The verb ērōtēsen (petitioned) is the same word used for prayer-petitions in the farewell discourses.
39: μίγμα σμύρνης καὶ ἀλόης ὡς λίτρας ἑκατόν -- approximately seventy-five pounds. The quantity is extraordinary -- royal burial quantities. Nicodemus, introduced in 3:1 as coming to Jesus by night (nyktós), now comes openly. The Evangelist's interest in burial details -- quantity and quality of spices -- underlines the honoring of Jesus as a king, counterpointing the mockery of John 18-19.
40: ὀθονίοις μετὰ τῶν ἀρωμάτων -- "linen cloths with the spices" -- the same noun othonia used for the burial cloths that Peter and the Beloved Disciple will find in John 20:5-7. The Evangelist's precise terminology here prepares the careful reader for the significance of the arrangement of those cloths in the empty tomb.
41: κῆπος -- "garden" (kēpos). Only John names the tomb location as within a garden. The garden setting at the end of the passion narrative echoes the garden of John 18:1 (where Jesus was arrested) and carries the faint resonance of Eden -- a reading the Evangelist enables but does not press.
42: διὰ τὴν παρασκευὴν τῶν Ἰουδαίων -- "because of the Jews' preparation" -- the burial is done hastily in a nearby tomb. The new tomb (kainós, unused) is not a detail of poverty but of honor: a tomb used for no previous person is fitting for the one who had claimed no previous person came down from heaven (John 3:13).
